attrs.alt: DOM Selection and Manipulation -- key APIs for selecting, traversing, and modifying the DOM

content.text: (DOM) is the browser's in-memory tree representation of an HTML page. Every HTML element, text node, and attribute is a node in this tree. JavaScript can read and modify it, and the browser reflects changes instantly in the rendered page.

Understanding the DOM directly is foundational — even though Angular's template engine handles most DOM updates for you, Angular directives, custom decorators, and lower-level performance work all interact with the DOM through the APIs covered here.
The two most versatile selection methods accept any valid CSS selector string.
— the element may not exist in the DOM at query time. TypeScript will warn you if you access properties without a null check.
These older APIs are marginally faster than
because they don't parse a selector string, but they're less flexible.
if you truly need to render HTML from an external source.
is the modern API for managing CSS classes. It replaces the old pattern of reading/writing

const btn = document.querySelector<HTMLButtonElement>('#submit')!;

btn.classList.add('loading');             // add one class
btn.classList.add('active', 'visible');   // add multiple at once
btn.classList.remove('hidden');
btn.classList.toggle('open');             // add if absent, remove if present
btn.classList.toggle('dark', isDark);     // force add/remove based on boolean
btn.classList.replace('loading', 'done'); // swap one class for another

console.log(btn.classList.contains('active')); // true/false
console.log([...btn.classList]); // array of current class names
Inline styles have the highest CSS specificity and are hard to override with stylesheets. Prefer toggling CSS classes; only use
for values that are computed at runtime and cannot be expressed as a class.
Every DOM insertion triggers a reflow. When inserting many elements, batch them in a
first — a lightweight container that is not part of the live DOM — then insert the fragment once.
Angular's template engine handles the vast majority of DOM updates declaratively. Direct DOM access should be a last resort.
When direct DOM access is needed: Renderer2
works in non-browser environments (SSR with Angular Universal, Web Workers) and is the correct abstraction when you genuinely need imperative DOM access.
Querying the DOM before it is ready
querySelector returns null; script runs before <body>
Place scripts before </body>, or use DOMContentLoaded
Use textContent, or sanitise with DOMPurify
Using live HTMLCollections in a mutation loop
Infinite loop or skipped elements as collection updates
Convert to Array.from() snapshot before iterating
Reading DOM layout properties in a tight loop
Batch reads first, then writes; use requestAnimationFrame
After selecting an element you often need to read its size, position, or the values of CSS properties. Be aware that reading layout properties forces the browser to calculate the current layout (
), so avoid doing it inside loops.
element holds inert markup that can be cloned into the live document. It is the foundation of web components and is useful for creating reusable DOM structures without string parsing.
Angular uses a virtual DOM test environment (JSDOM via Karma / jsdom in Jest). You can assert on the DOM without a browser.
DOM selection and manipulation are the foundation of all JavaScript-powered interactivity. Key points:
querySelector / querySelectorAll accept any CSS selector and return static results
getElementById and getElementsBy* return live collections -- be careful in mutation loops
Prefer classList.add/remove/toggle over inline styles for state-driven appearance changes
Always use textContent for text; only use innerHTML with trusted, static HTML
Batch DOM insertions in a DocumentFragment to trigger only one reflow
In Angular, use template bindings ([class], [attr], [style]) first; reach for Renderer2 or ElementRef only when required
Never assign user-supplied HTML to innerHTML -- sanitise it first
